Increasing tongue exercises such as sliding or rolling your tongue can help promote tongue and throat health and improve breathing quality during sleep.
Your breathing quality last night was %1$s, which was a little low. Congestion can negatively impact breathing quality during sleep.

Nasal irritation or discomfort may affect the breathing quality during sleep. Avoid pollen, dust, and other allergens, wear a mask when it is smoggy or cold, regularly cleaning the nasal passage, and massaging sinus points are all effective means of prevention.
Your breathing quality last night was only %1$s.
Choosing an appropriate pillow that supports your neck can help to improve your breathing quality during sleep. A pillow that is not suited for you may lead to airway blockage.
Your breathing quality last night was %1$s, which was a little low. Fatigue can negatively impact breathing quality during sleep.
Fatigue can cause irregular breathing and snoring during sleep. A good work-life balance and regular rest can help.
Your breathing quality last night was %1$s, which is below the standard level.
Weight gain negatively impacts breathing quality during sleep. A healthy diet and regular exercise can prevent weight gain.
The breathing quality of your sleep last night was %1$s. This past week your breathing quality has been lower than normal.

Snoring is a symptom of poor breathing during sleep. Snoring may lead to a lack of oxygen to the brain, memory loss, a lack of deep sleep, and high blood pressure. Avoid smoking and drinking alcohol if you snore, as these can worsen respiratory symptoms and exacerbate irregular breathing during sleep.
The breathing quality of your sleep last night was %1$s. This past week your breathing quality has been lower than normal.

Snoring indicates poor breathing quality during sleep. If you have a history of breathing with your mouth during sleep, waking up often during the night, and feeling tired after sleep, you may want to consult a medical professional.
A blocked nose, a cold, or a sore throat can affect your breathing quality. Exercising may help reduce your chance of snoring.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your sleep last night. That\'s about normal.
Dreams mostly occur during REM sleep. Maintaining regular REM sleep can help promote creativity and relieve stress.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your sleep last night. That\'s about normal.
People can usually recount their dreams if they wake up during the REM sleep stage. The longer the gap between REM sleep and waking, the harder dreams are to remember.

Dreams mostly occur during REM sleep. Dreaming does not indicate poor sleep quality, rather it satisfies a normal biological need. Remembering dreams often can indicate inadequate deep sleep time. Regular exercise can promote deep sleep and improve sleep quality.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your overall sleep last night, which was a little high. Relaxing before bed can help improve sleep quality.
Dreams mostly occur during REM sleep. Controlling stress caused by work and avoiding overthinking and fatigue can help improve sleep quality.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your overall sleep last night, which was a little high. Relaxing before bed can help improve sleep quality.
Too much REM sleep can cause you to wake up still tired. Practice breathing exercises before sleep for more balanced REM cycles and improved sleep quality.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your overall sleep last night, which was a little high, but overall sleep quality was good.
Dreams usually occur during REM sleep. Try writing down your dreams after you wake up, focusing on your feelings, who was present, the location, and events.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your overall sleep last night, which was a little high. Exercise can help improve sleep quality.

Too many dreams can indicate that REM sleep accounts for too much of overall sleep. Running can help you prevent getting too much REM sleep. For best results, use your wearable device to create a running schedule.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your overall sleep last night, which was a little high. Going to bed earlier can help improve sleep quality.

Dreams occur during REM sleep, and REM cycles occur more often in the second half of your sleep. Too much REM sleep can cause fatigue. If you dream often, adjust your schedule so that you sleep earlier and wake up earlier.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your overall sleep last night, which was a little low.
REM sleep is important for creativity and mood regulation. Missing out on REM sleep occasionally is fine, as it will not negatively impact your health and your body catches up within a few nights.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your overall sleep last night, which was a little low. REM sleep can help promote creativity.

REM sleep, the phase during which most dreams occur, is important for creativity, mood regulation, and stress relief. Studies show that staying away from caffeine, nicotine, and alcohol improves the quality of REM sleep.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your overall sleep last night, which was a little low, but overall sleep quality was good.

REM sleep, the phase during which most dreams occur, is important for creativity and mood regulation. REM occurs more often in the second half of your sleep. To get more REM sleep, increase your total sleep time by a reasonable amount.
REM sleep accounted for %1$s of your overall sleep last night, which was a little low, but overall sleep quality was good.

REM sleep satisfies a normal biological need and is associated with dreaming. Missing out on REM sleep occasionally is fine, as it will not negatively impact your health and your body catches up within a few nights.
You slept for %1$s last night. Your sleep quality was good.
Normal sleep architecture includes REM (rapid eye movement) and NREM (non-rapid eye movement) sleep. NREM sleep includes light sleep and deep sleep, and REM sleep is usually associated with dreaming.
You slept for %1$s last night. The quality of your sleep was okay.

Most people who suffer from poor sleep quality have poor sleeping habits. These include irregular lifestyle habits, a lack of adequate sunlight, a lack of exercise, or frequent consumption and use of coffee, alcohol and cigarettes.

GPS locating is the phone\'s positioning chip receiving satellite signals and using them to locate its position. It is restricted by factors such as the environment and time it takes to receive the signals. In places with poor satellite signals (such as indoors, tunnels, and urban areas with high-rise buildings), GPS signals can be weak or non-existent. Without GPS, you can\'t accurately record data. To fix this, you can try:
%d. Checking that Location services are enabled on the phone.
%d. Moving to a more open area with a better satellite signal.
%d. Turning off any power saving features on the phone. These limit how often the device obtains the GPS signal and may affect data.

In progressive overload training, when starting at a lower intensity, the body\'s oxygen intake is much larger than the demand. During this time you mainly rely on aerobic metabolism. As the intensity increases, oxygen uptake is less than the oxygen demand and your aerobic metabolism isn\'t able to keep up. During this time, your anaerobic metabolism starts to improve and lactic acid is produced at an accelerated rate. As the load increases at a certain point lactate starts to accumulate faster than it can be removed, your heart rate and pace at this point are your lactate-threshold heart rate and pace.
What is lactate-threshold heart rate and pace?

In progressive overload training, when starting at a lower intensity, the body\'s oxygen intake is much larger than the demand. During this time you mainly rely on your aerobic metabolism. As the intensity increases, oxygen uptake is less than the oxygen demand and your aerobic metabolism isn\'t able to keep up. During this time, you start to rely more on your anaerobic metabolism, the part it plays continues to increase, and lactic acid is also produced at accelerated rates. As the load increases, at a certain point lactate starts to accumulate faster than it can be removed. This is called your lactate threshold, your heart rate and pace at this point are your lactate-threshold heart rate and pace.

Running in this zone is recommended after heavy training. It can get rid of fatigue and help with muscle recovery. This zone is also recommended after training at a moderate intensity for an extended period of time.

Running in this zone should feel relaxed, breathing should be steady, and it should be able to be sustained for a good period of time. This intensity is good for increasing your muscles\' ability to metabolize oxygen as well as improve your cardio fitness and aerobic metabolism.
Running in this zone should be more difficult, breathing should be slightly faster, but the body should still be able to adjust and stabilize easily. This is good for long distance training.

Running in this zone should be difficult, breathing should be faster, lactic acid will be produced faster, and the body will increasingly metabolize it to maintain a balance. Training at this level is good for improving lactate tolerance and threshold as well as anaerobic endurance.

Running in this zone should be uncomfortable, breathing should be short, and it should be difficult to maintain for a long period of time. This intensity is best suited for high intensity interval training and helps with increasing maximum speed and sprinting performance.

Monitoring your SpO₂ levels at higher altitudes is important in staying safe. SpO₂ levels are lower at higher altitudes because the ability for oxygen to enter your bloodstream is reduced due to lower atmospheric pressure. At extremely high altitudes, lower SpO₂ levels are more apparent and can be accompanied by symptoms of altitude adaptation decline at altitudes over %d meters.
%1$d. Hypothermia can cause your blood vessels and arteries to narrow, reducing blood perfusion. Temperatures below %2$d°C can affect data accuracy and the ability to provide results.

%d. Living or traveling in regions with extremely high altitudes can have a big impact on people physically. Monitor your body\'s response closely. Seek professional assistance immediately if you start to feel uncomfortable.
%d. To ensure accurate results, use this feature in the specified temperature and altitude ranges.
The air is thinner at high altitudes, which reduces the ability of oxygen to enter the bloodstream. Not getting enough oxygen could increase your risk of altitude adaptation decline.
%1$d. Declined adaptation evaluation results pertain to the next %2$d hours. If you start to feel uncomfortable, it\'s suggested to assess your physical condition immediately.
%d. People feel uncomfortable when exposed to low-pressure, low-oxygen environments. Make sure to pay more attention to your SpO₂ in these climates.
%1$d. Scores are based on the %2$d Lake Louise Acute Mountain Sickness Score. Declined adaptation evaluation results are for reference only.

Imbalance puts more stress on one leg than the other. Running like this for a long time could increase your chances of injury. To prevent this, do Bulgarian split squats and alternating lunge jumps to increase your balance and lower-body strength.

Ground contact time balance is the balance between the time each foot is in contact with the ground while running. It reflects your symmetry. An ideal value is between %1$s–%2$s with the left and right values close to each other. Long-term imbalance increases the chances of injury.
You can improve your ability to absorb impact by doing lower-body strength training.

Active peak is the maximum impact of your foot hitting the ground. The better your body is at absorbing shock, the lower the value will be. Active peak is closely related to speed, the faster you are the harder the impact is. Ground impact is measured in units of BW (body weight). For example, %1$s BW means the landing force on your foot is twice your body weight.

A higher vertical oscillation indicates a lot of energy is being wasted on vertical movement rather than forward movement. You can jump in place (alternating which leg is in front) and do high knees to practice keeping your center of gravity low.
Vertical oscillation is how much your center of gravity changes vertically as you run. Generally speaking, a higher value means less efficient running.

A higher vertical stride ratio means that assuming the same stride length, you are using more energy to go up than forward. You can jump in place (alternating which leg is in front) and do alternating lunge jumps to work on the elasticity of your Achilles tendon and improve your ratio.

Vertical stride ratio = Vertical oscillation (cm) ÷ Stride (cm), it represents running efficiency. For two people with the same stride length, the one with a higher vertical stride ratio is running less efficiently.
You can practice calf raises, high knees, and other exercises to increase your lower body strength or wear shoes that have softer soles to help absorb the shock.

Impact loading rate is the slope of the curve between initial contact and the impact peak (roughly %1$s–%2$s between these points). The higher the impact loading rate, the quicker the impact force rises. If your foot isn\'t absorbing the shock well, it increases the risk of injury.

The difference between pre and post meal blood sugar levels is the absolute difference between blood sugar measured before eating and %1$d hours after eating. The pre and post meal measurements must be for the same meal and taken on the same day for this data. The ideal difference is less than %2$.1f mmol/L.

Current blood sugar level is normal. To make sure that your meal didn\'t have any negative effects on your blood sugar, it\'s important to get in the habit of monitoring your blood sugar %d hours before and after eating.

Current blood sugar level is very low. It is important to eat %1$d-%2$d g of sugary snacks as soon as possible. If your blood sugar continues to drop, seek medical attention immediately.
Current blood sugar level is low. Avoid hyper-controlling your food intake. Wait until your blood sugar returns to normal before exercising, otherwise your blood sugar could drop too low.
Current blood sugar level is high. Please pay attention to your food intake and exercise reasonably to lower your blood sugar.

Sleep efficiency is a key indicator for measuring sleep quality. Generally, higher efficiency indicates better sleep quality. The formula for calculating sleep efficiency is: Sleep efficiency = (Sleep duration/Time in bed) × %1$s.\n· Normal (%2$s–%3$s): Good sleep is usually an efficiency of %4$s or above.\n· Low (< %5$s): Poor sleeping habits or insomnia can lead to an efficiency of less than %6$s, this often goes hand-in-hand with troubles sleeping such as difficulty falling asleep, sleeping lightly, and waking up too early.

· Stick to a routine, try to go to bed and get up at the same time every day.
· Avoid drinking strong tea or coffee before bed.
· Eat properly and avoid eating too much just before bed.
· Relax and avoid overstimulating your brain before bed.
· Don't try to work out important things just before bed, it can increase stress and anxiety.
· Avoid doing intense exercise %s hours before bed.
· Keep the lighting soft.
· Only lie down when you're tired.
· Avoid playing with electronics right before bed.
(%1$d) Time in bed is from when you lie down in bed to when you get up. For most people, this is when you turn off the lights to when you wake up.
(%1$d) For insomniacs it\'s important to maintain a schedule. Insomniacs usually make up sleep by staying in bed longer, but this only decreases sleep efficiency, creating a vicious cycle.

Time in bed is the time from when you lay down in bed to when you get up. %1$d. How can sleep latency be improved?

Sleep latency is the time it takes you to fall asleep after turning the lights out. On average, a healthy adult takes %1$d–%2$d minutes to fall asleep. If it takes longer than %3$d minutes, it could be a sign of insomnia.

Study shows that sleep latency could be affected by a variety of factors, such as alcohol, pains, sleep environment, emotions, and naps you take during the day. Developing healthy sleep habits helps you fall asleep more easily and improve sleep quality (e.g., keeping a balanced diet, creating a good sleep environment, restricting naps, exercising and relaxing more).

Getting enough sleep is important for improving memory and learning performance.
Getting good quality sleep helps regulate hunger hormones and suppress the formation of fat cells, reducing the risk of obesity.
Good sleep helps with insulin secretion, and is important for maintaining healthy blood sugar levels.
Quality sleep is good for mood regulation, helping you relax and preventing negative emotions.
Good quality sleep is vital for tissue and cell repair and ensures you feel energized the next day.
Skin cell turnover is most efficient during sleep. Getting enough sleep helps keep your skin elastic and glowing.
Good sleep is essential for eye health, helping you maintain your vision.
Good quality sleep helps boost your immune system, cognitive performance, and creativity.
The body\'s basal metabolic rate is relatively low during sleep, making it the perfect time for your body to recover and get rid of fatigue.
Good sleep helps boost your immune system, removing pathogens from your body and reducing the risk of illness.
Quality sleep helps your body deliver nutrients to your skin, keeping it elastic and glowing.
Getting good quality sleep helps you feel energetic and refreshed, increasing productivity.
Quality sleep helps cancel out negative emotions that have built up during the day, helping you wake up feeling more refreshed and ready for a new day.
The metabolic rates of organs drop during sleep, allowing your tissues to rest and your body to recover from the stress of the day.
Quality sleep allows you to rest, recover, and recharge, both physically and mentally.
Good sleep helps your central nervous system relax and relieves stress.
Good sleep helps you balance your leptin and ghrelin levels, which helps with weight control.
Sleep helps your brain clear out any \"junk\" that has accumulated from your daily activities so that you can wake up feeling refreshed the next day.
Sleep helps with protein synthesis, cell renewal, and immune system recovery.
Good sleep helps boost your immune system, protecting you from germs and bacteria.
Sleep stimulates the pituitary gland to release growth hormones faster and accelerates cells\' metabolism, helping your body recover.
Excessive dreaming for multiple nights in a row can make you feel more tired during the day, and even lead to palpitations, dizziness, limpness, and forgetfulness.
Excessive dreaming for multiple nights in a row can lead to neurasthenia and a decline in memory.
Excessive dreaming for multiple nights in a row can lead to poor sleep quality and make you feel tired during the day.
Excessive dreaming makes you feel more tired when you wake up, prevents your body from recovering, and leads to reduced concentration and forgetfulness.
Excessive dreaming makes you wake up feeling tired and leads to poor concentration, forgetfulness, and other symptoms, compromising your daytime productivity.

Repeatedly waking up in the middle of the night over a long period of time puts more stress on your heart and may trigger high blood pressure, a-fib, coronary heart disease, and other cardiovascular diseases.
Repeatedly waking up in the middle of the night for multiple nights in a row can lead to depression, anxiety, and other negative emotions and affect your sleep quality, creating a vicious cycle.
Waking up often in the middle of the night can affect your memory and concentration, and increase your risk of obesity and heart disease.
If you keep waking up frequently during the night it could prevent your body from being able to fully recover, and can even lead to chronic fatigue syndrome.
Waking up easily at night can affect your ability to control your emotions and cause drastic mood swings.
Waking up easily in the middle of the night can lead to inadequate sleep, causing dark circles and wrinkles around your eyes, and even accelerate aging.
Insufficient REM sleep can disrupt your mental balance and make it easy for you to experience irritability, anxiety, and other negative emotions.
Insufficient REM sleep can affect your mental state and make you wake up feeling tired mentally and physically.
Long-term lack of deep sleep can cause sleepiness during the day and affect your memory, concentration, and mood.
Long-term lack of deep sleep can reduce blood flow to your brain, leading to the accelerated aging of brain cells, and affecting your ability to think.
Long-term lack of deep sleep can contribute to metabolic disorders and even increase your risk of diabetes and other metabolic diseases.
Lack of deep sleep can slow down your metabolism and accelerate the aging process.
Lack of deep sleep can affect your skin, leaving it dry and dull, and even leading to premature age spots.
Not getting enough deep sleep can affect your metabolism and lead to premature skin aging.
Hypersomnia can slow down blood circulation and reduce the amount of oxygen your muscles can get from your blood, making it easier to feel lethargic.
Hypersomnia can reduce the body\'s metabolic rate, making it easier to feel fatigued or drowsy upon waking.
Hypersomnia can interrupt your regular sleeping schedule and upset your biological clock, and may cause insomnia.
Going to bed late can weaken your immune system, making it easier to catch a cold or trigger allergies.
Frequently staying up late can lead to endocrine disorders, nerve system disorders, and skin problems.
Staying up late can overload your eyes, cause eye strain, and cause your eyesight to decline over time.
Frequently experiencing sleep interruptions over a long period of time can disrupt your endocrine system, affect insulin secretion, and increase your risk of diabetes.
Frequently experiencing sleep interruptions over a long period of time can increase your chances of catching a cold or experiencing gastrointestinal issues, obesity, and high blood sugar.
Frequently experiencing sleep interruptions over a long period of time can promote atherosclerosis, increasing your risk of developing cardiovascular and brain diseases.
Interrupted sleep prevents your brain from processing and storing memories from the day, leading to a decline in memory performance.
Interrupted sleep can leave you feeling tired and lethargic, and affect your memory.
Interrupted sleep can disrupt the balance between leptin and ghrelin, making it easy to become over- or underweight.
Long-term sleep deprivation can weaken your immune system and lead to neurasthenia and gastrointestinal illnesses.
Long-term sleep deprivation can lead to endocrine disorders, an imbalance in hormones, and intense mood swings.
Sleep deprivation makes you feel tired even just after waking up and leads to skin problems, such as dull skin, acne, and patchy skin.
Sleep deprivation can lead to daytime drowsiness, which affects your performance at work or school.
Frequently waking up before planned can be a sign of a sleep disorder and can increase your risk of obesity and cardiovascular diseases.
Low sleep efficiency can speed up collagen loss, making your skin look dull and increasing wrinkles.
Low sleep efficiency can affect your concentration and problem-solving capabilities, reducing your productivity.
Low sleep efficiency can lead to depression and anxiety and inhibit stress relief.
Low sleep efficiency can lead to reduced concentration and alertness.

New data such as time in bed, sleep latency, sleep efficiency, and heart rate, SpO2, and respiratory rate during sleep.
This is when your body enters sleep.
This refers to when your body wakes up. If you wake up multiple times throughout the night, this is the final time you woke up.
This is when you physically get out of bed. Time you spend lying in bed doing other things, such as reading a book, won\'t be included.
This is what time you get in bed. Time you spend lying in bed doing other things, such as reading a book, won\'t be included.
%1$s. How to wake up at a consistent time

%1$s) Go to bed at the same time every day and avoid stimulating your brain just before bed. Relax, be calm, and avoid overthinking. Avoid caffeine and nicotine, these excite your nervous system making it hard to fall asleep.\n%2$s) No matter how much sleep you got the night before, it\'s important to wake up at the same time every day. This brings order to your routine and helps you feel fresh every day.
This is the measure of how regular your wake-up time is over a period of time.
This is the measure of how regular the time you go to sleep is over a period of time.

Going to bed and getting up at the same time every day can strengthen your circadian rhythm. A stable biological clock also stabilizes your metabolism and keeps your mind fresh. Staying up late and lying in bed for too long mess up your circadian rhythm and can affect your mood.

Sleep efficiency is a key measure of sleep quality. Generally higher sleep efficiency indicates higher sleep quality.
Formula:
Sleep efficiency = (Sleep duration ÷ Time in bed) × %1$s
A sleep efficiency of %2$s or above is good, less than %3$s could be due to problems falling asleep, waking up frequently, or other sleep issues.

Improving your sleep efficiency can improve sleep quality. If your sleep efficiency is low, try the following:
%1$s) Form a routine to strengthen your circadian rhythm. Make sure to get sunlight exposure during the day, avoid napping for too long, and be careful what time you fall asleep, and go to bed at a scheduled time at night.
%2$s) Avoid overstimulating your brain, extreme emotions, or overthinking before bed, make sure to relax and be calm.
%3$s) Recognize good sleep, don't magnify or misinterpret poor sleep. If you have troubles falling asleep, don't be anxious or repeatedly check the time. Simply relax and trust that you can fall asleep.
%4$s) Create a quiet comfortable sleeping environment, with an appropriate temperature and brightness.
%5$s) Be active during the day, go for a brisk walk or jog. However, make sure not to do this right before bed, otherwise your body will be left in an excited state making it hard to fall asleep.
%6$s) Avoid caffeine and nicotine, these excite your nervous system making it hard to fall asleep.
%7$s) Make sure your body associates bed with sleep. Try not to lie in bed playing with your phone, reading, or other activities. It's best to use your bedroom just for sleeping.

This is the duration of time from when you get in bed to when you get out of bed. For most people, this is when you get in bed and turn off the lights to when you get out of bed in the morning. Time you spend lying in bed doing other things, such as reading a book, won\'t be included.\nMaintaining a healthy in bed time is important. People with insomnia always try to make up for it by lying in bed, however, this can actually reduce sleep efficiency and make the symptoms worse.

The reasons for waking up during the night can be complex. These interruptions range from a brief moment to a longer period of time. You might not even be aware that you woke up. In general, you shouldn\'t wake up more than %1$s times during the night, and the time awake shouldn\'t be more than %2$s of total sleep. If you wake up frequently, you may have sleep-maintenance insomnia, in which case the ratio of deep sleep may be lower than others. Even if the total amount of sleep you get doesn\'t change, you won\'t wake up feeling as refreshed and recovered.\nThe times you get up from your bed while still awake, for example before falling asleep or after waking up, don\'t count.

%1$s) Healthy adults are generally able to maintain a stable respiratory rate while asleep. However, fatigue or upper airway issues can affect your ability to breathe, cause snoring, hypopnea, breathing obstruction, and other issues. These not only affect sleep quality, if it prolongs, it can increase the risk of heart disease.\n%2$s) Healthy adults who experience less than %3$s hypopnea events per hour generally have a sleep breathing quality score of %4$s points or above. %5$s or more hypopnea events per hour generally mean a sleep breathing quality score of %6$s points or less.

If you only have poor sleep quality when you are fatigued or after drinking, accompanied with light, even snoring, it's generally not a big problem.
If it occurs frequently, try sleeping on your side, avoid smoking and drinking, and increase exercise during the day to help improve sleep quality. It's also important to maintain a healthy weight.
If it becomes an ongoing problem and you experience other symptoms, make an appointment to see an otorhinolaryngologist or respiratory specialist. Symptoms include:
%1$s) Waking up frequently or getting up to go to the bathroom a lot.
%2$s) Waking up tired or with low spirits.
%3$s) Waking up with a dry mouth, acid reflux, heartburn, or other symptoms.
%4$s) Loud or uneven snoring, or interruptions in breathing.
%5$s) Drowsiness during the day or reduced ability to focus.
Disabling this will also disable Sleep apnea.

This is your heart rate while asleep. For healthy adults this is generally slightly lower than resting heart rate (%1$s–%2$s bpm).

This is blood oxygen saturation (SpO2) while asleep. Blood oxygen saturation (SpO2) is the percentage of hemoglobin in the blood that contains oxygen – in other words, the amount of oxygen in the blood.\nFor healthy adults SpO2 is usually between %1$s–%2$s and is slightly lower during sleep.
This is breaths per minute while asleep. For healthy adults this is generally slightly lower than resting respiratory rate (%1$s–%2$s brpm).

This is the amount of time it takes to enter sleep after lying down. If you lie in bed doing other things, such as read a book, this time isn\'t included.\nHealthy adults\' sleep latency is generally no more than %1$s minutes. More than %2$s minutes means you\'re having trouble falling asleep and could affect sleep quality.

There are many things that could lead to a longer sleep latency. Try the following to help you fall asleep faster:
%1$s) Form a routine to strengthen your circadian rhythm. Make sure to get sunlight exposure during the day, avoid napping for too long, and be careful what time you fall asleep, and go to bed at a scheduled time at night.
%2$s) Avoid overstimulating your brain, extreme emotions, or overthinking before bed, make sure to relax and be calm.
%3$s) Recognize good sleep, don't magnify or misinterpret poor sleep. If you have troubles falling asleep, don't be anxious or repeatedly check the time. Simply relax and trust that you can fall asleep.
%4$s) Create a quiet comfortable sleeping environment, with an appropriate temperature and brightness.
%5$s) Be active during the day, go for a brisk walk or jog. However, make sure not to do this right before bed, otherwise your body will be left in an excited state making it hard to fall asleep.
%6$s) Avoid caffeine and nicotine, these excite your nervous system making it hard to fall asleep.
%7$s) Make sure your body associates bed with sleep. Try not to lie in bed playing with your phone, reading, or other activities. It's best to use your bedroom just for sleeping.
A hot bath before bed can relax your body, help you fall asleep faster, and improve sleep quality.
Large meals before bed can make your stomach and intestines work overtime to digest all that food, which can keep you up at night and affect sleep quality.
Choose a moderately soft pillow that contours to your natural neck and shoulder curve and stop possible sleep apnea.
Good sleep helps boost your immune system, protecting you from germs and bacteria.
Sleep stimulates the pituitary gland to release growth hormones faster and accelerates cells\' metabolism, helping your body recover.

What is average sleep heart rate?

This indicates your average heart rate (HR) when sleeping at night. Generally, this value will be lower when compared to your heart rate when awake and should be %s bpm or below. A lower sleep heart rate can be a sign of a stronger and healthier cardiovascular system. However, physical changes, higher altitudes, and disease could cause it to be higher. A higher average for a long period of time is linked to a greater risk of disease.

This is a personal benchmark range derived from your average heart rate data when asleep. Your data should fall within this range if you haven\'t gone through any lifestyle, physical, or environmental changes. Long term changes can affect your personal range.
A value higher than your personal range can be caused by an illness, an increased intake of caffeine/alcohol, being at a higher altitude, or other factors.
A value lower than your personal range can be caused by an improvement in lifestyle habits, such as avoiding alcohol/caffeine and going to bed earlier, or recovering from an illness.
What is average sleep respiratory rate?

This indicates your average respiratory rate when sleeping at night. Generally, this value will be lower when compared to your respiratory rate when awake. A higher sleep respiratory rate can be a sign of hypoxia, stress, and more.

This is a personal benchmark range derived from your average respiratory rate data when asleep. Your data should fall within this range if you haven\'t gone through any lifestyle, physical, or environmental changes. Long term changes can affect your personal range.
A value higher than your personal range can be caused by illness, increased intake of caffeine/alcohol, being at a higher altitude, or other factors.
A value lower than your personal range can be caused by an illness, being at a higher altitude, or other factors.
What is average sleep heart rate variability (HRV)?

This indicates your average heart rate variability (HRV) when sleeping at night. Generally, this value will be higher when compared to your HRV when awake. A higher sleep heart rate variability can be a sign of physical relaxation and indicate faster stress relief. Whereas a lower value can be a sign of stress or fatigue, and indicate slower stress relief.

This is a personal benchmark range derived from your average heart rate variability data when asleep. Your data should fall within this range if you haven\'t gone through any lifestyle, physical, or environmental changes. Long term changes can affect your personal range.
A value higher than your personal range can be caused by an improvement in lifestyle habits, such as avoiding alcohol/caffeine and going to bed earlier, or recovery from an illness.
A value lower than your personal range can be caused by an illness, an increased intake of caffeine/alcohol, increased stress, or other factors.

This indicates your average blood oxygen saturation (SpO₂) when sleeping at night. Usually, this should be %s or above. A higher value means your blood has a higher capacity to carry oxygen. However, physical changes, higher altitudes, and disease could cause it to be lower. A lower average for a long period of time is linked to a higher risk of disease.

This is a personal benchmark range derived from your average SpO₂ data when asleep. Your data should fall within this range if you haven\'t gone through any lifestyle, physical, or environmental changes. Long term changes can affect your personal range.
A value lower than your personal range can be caused by an improvement in lifestyle habits, such as avoiding alcohol/caffeine and going to bed earlier, or recovery from an illness.

This indicates your average respiratory rate when sleeping at night. Generally, this is %1$s–%2$s brpm and is slightly lower compared to your respiratory rate when awake. A higher sleep respiratory rate can be a sign of hypoxia, poor health, stress, and more.

This indicates your average heart rate variability (HRV) when sleeping at night. HRV reflects small variations in time between heart beats and is a key indicator in assessing physical health and recovery. HRV is typically %1$s–%2$s ms and is generally higher during sleep than when you\'re awake. HRV is affected by age, sex, and personal health, resulting in significant differences between individuals. Studies show a higher sleep HRV can be a sign of greater physical activity, better sleep, and good health, whereas a lower value can be a sign of stress or poor health. Wear your wearable device more often to keep track of changes in your sleep HRV.

Event manager allows you to add official and custom running events, sync them to your watch, and set distances and goal times. It requires the following information.
This includes event name, location, time, distance, goal time, route, and more. Events can be found in our database or customized to suit your own needs.

Your approximate location is used to recommend nearby events when you have granted location access for the Health app (granted separately). You can still search for events manually even if you don\'t grant location access. Location information won\'t be stored on our servers.
Smart Training Plan can provide personalized training programs that adapt in real time based on sports science principles and your personal information. It requires the following information.
This includes heart rate, heart rate variability, sleep data, breathing data, stress levels, blood oxygen, body fat percentage, and menstrual cycle data.

This includes speed, workout heart rate, workout duration, workout route, workout distance and ascent, workout performance, cadence, workout type, calories, workout status (running ability index, training load, recovery time, etc.), running form data, VO2Max, personal preferences, other workout data, and fitness courses and plans.

This includes date of birth, sex, weight, and height. Weight and height information is mandatory as it\'ll be used to determine your body mass index (BMI) so as to provide safe, suitable workout courses for you along with effective fitness tips. In addition, your age and sex are used to ensure better training results throughout the program. If this information is inaccurate, the algorithm might not provide workout courses or fitness tips accurate to you and that may increase your risk of injury.

Your location information is used to assess weather, temperature, humidity, and UV index data when you have granted location access for the Health app (granted separately). You can still use this feature even if you don\'t grant location access, but results might be less accurate. Location information won\'t be stored on our servers. Elevation information will also be used to assess fitness adaptation.
This includes event name, location, time, distance, and goal performance.

During deep sleep (N3), your breathing, blood pressure, and brain waves slow down noticeably. It\'s the most restorative stage of sleep but also the hardest to wake up from. If disturbed, you may feel dizzy, confused, groggy, or disoriented. Studies have found that deep sleep is crucial for physical and mental recovery, as well as body-mind balance. Longer periods of deep sleep often indicate higher-quality rest.

During light sleep (N1/N2), your body begins to relax, with your breathing and heart rate slowing down, brain activity calming, and body temperature decreasing. It\'s easy to wake up from this stage of sleep, which acts as a natural defense mechanism essential to health and safety. Light sleep is just as important for your body as other sleep stages are. However, spending too much time in this stage usually indicates low-quality sleep, leaving you feeling tired and less refreshed.

During REM (rapid eye movement) sleep, your eyes move rapidly back and forth. It\'s also when most dreaming happens. Waking up during this stage is tougher than during light sleep, but easier than during deep sleep. Muscles are usually paralyzed during REM sleep, which acts as a safety mechanism to prevent acting out in dreams. That\'s why you might feel like you can\'t run or shout in dreams. Studies have shown that REM sleep plays a key role in strengthening memories and managing emotions.
Get a complete map of your sleep from TruSleep™ by going to bed with your Huawei wearable on.

Tips for getting more stable sleep

Stable sleep is characterized by steady, well-coordinated breathing and heart rate. The better this is, the better you sleep. This is essential for fatigue relief, physical recovery, and recharging yourself.

To address sleep issues, professional guidelines suggest starting with cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), a proven method focused on changing negative thoughts and unhealthy behaviors. This recommendation is supported by clinical practice, which shows that chronic insomnia is often linked to poor sleep habits.\nIf you\'re looking to boost your sleep quality and enhance sleep stability, here are some practical tips to try.

%s. Manage stress and mood: Aim for emotional balance and find ways to reduce anxiety, stress, and feelings of depression. Staying calm and avoiding overexcitement before bed can set the stage for a more stable sleep.
%s. Listen to your body: Avoid pushing yourself to exhaustion. Physical discomfort like pain, itching, or coughing can disrupt sleep stability, so take care of these symptoms if they arise.
%s. Optimize your environment: Create a sleep-friendly space that\'s quiet, dark, and at a comfortable temperature and humidity level. The right mattress and bedding can also make a big difference.

%s. Stick to a sleep routine: Go to bed and wake up about the same time every day to sync with your natural body clock. Changing work shifts, an irregular work/rest schedule, or jet lag can throw off your rhythm and prevent you getting stable sleep.

%s. Watch what you eat and drink: Follow a balanced diet and limit stimulants like caffeine, alcohol, nicotine, and heavy meals, especially in the evening. Cutting out coffee and tea after midday can help a lot, too.

%s. Stay active: An appropriate amount of exercise encourages stable sleep, as it helps your body recover from the physical load. Just be mindful not to do intense workouts too close to bedtime, as they may make it harder to fall asleep.

%s. Enable the Sleep apnea feature on your watch and have it detect pauses in your breathing during sleep: Sleep apnea often disrupts sleep continuity and stability by causing frequent awakenings. If this happens, consider adjusting your lifestyle habits. Sleeping on your side may help minimize breathing interruptions. Maintaining a healthy weight can reduce excess tissue around the neck that might obstruct airflow. Additionally, avoid alcohol close to bedtime, cut back on smoking, and address nasal congestion – all these can further support better breathing patterns during sleep.
Try eating more foods rich in tryptophan, like soya products and meat, and foods rich in vitamin B6, such as bananas, potatoes, and chicken, to help with sleep.

Your jump rope streak is impressive.

Reflects long term aerobic endurance, and is the duration of time the heart and lungs are able to keep working during above average intensity exercise. Jump roping at a slow or moderate speed for an extended period of time can improve endurance and help burn fat.

Reflects the ability to maintain a high intensity for an extended period of time and is measured as the performance under the highest intensity. This can be improved by jump roping at a moderate to high speed for longer periods of time. This also improves aerobic ability, endurance, and glucose conversion efficiency.

Reflects the body\'s ability to quickly enter the anaerobic zone, and indicates the muscles and bones rapid response ability. This can be improved by jump roping really fast for short periods of time. This also improves speed endurance.
Reflects the amount of exercise and is measured by the total number of jumps. The load within a certain time frame indicates how much exercise the body can handle and recovery time.
Reflects rhythm and coordination abilities. The higher the number, the more impressive. This number can be improved by working on endurance and coordination.

Swing angle refers to the total amount the leg swings from push-off to the highest position as the lower leg folds up towards the thigh. A larger swing angle means a smaller motion radius, contributing to increased leg swing efficiency. This index usually increases with speed, and the common range is %1$d-%2$d°. Swing angle can be increased with targeted training such as butt kicks and hip bridge exercises. (Unit: degrees)

The body goes through different changes during the menstruation cycle. When enabled, Cycle prediction will combine data from your wearable such as resting heart rate, breathing rate, and changes in your body temperature to predict the fertile window.
The wearable can save up to %1$s days of data. It's recommended to keep the wearable connected to the phone to upload data and to wear it at night to improve accuracy.
*Predictions will be updated after adding or changing data on your phone. This could cause the fertile window to be extended or ovulation day to be unavailable. Wearing the device at night and regenerating the predictions will help recover the feature.

Health Clovers is a service provided by Huawei Health. This service guides you to cultivate healthy living habits. Due to differences between people, Health Clovers can only provide general recommendations for health improvements, especially regarding exercise recommendations, and the effects are not guaranteed. For those who are unable to exercise or require a doctor\'s approval to exercise, it is important to consult a medical organization or professional first and to follow their advice carefully. This is particularly important for children, the elderly, pregnant women, and those who have recently had surgery or are otherwise unable to engage in physical activity. Always follow the doctor\'s orders and avoid high intensity activity. If you feel uncomfortable at any time, stop immediately and rest or get medical assistance. The health advice provided by Health Clovers is for your understanding and reference only. You are responsible for all risks, damages, and responsibilities caused by participation in any activities or sports.

Before you start an activity or exercise, please read and understand this document carefully. By accepting the terms of the User Notice, you are signing and willing to abide by the contents of this document.

As a user of Huawei Health Beta version, we expect to receive feedback from you about this app. Your feedback allows us to improve the app before the release of the official version.

When you use the Beta user feedback feature in this app, we will collect your feedback including any files you attach and the app logs. These may include sensitive data from Huawei Health regarding your health (such as height, weight, BMI, heart rate, heart rate variability, emotional state, VO2Max, sleep, stress, menstrual cycle, skin temperature, ECG, arterial stiffness, blood oxygen, blood pressure, respiration, and body composition related data and metrics) and fitness (such as your workout location and route). The processed data depends on the services you are using, the capabilities of your wearable, and the data you actively input. %1$s The log file will also include personal profile, device and network, and usage information as defined in the %2$s.
By tapping AGREE, which is a prerequisite for using the Beta version, you consent to such processing of your health and fitness related data in connection with analyzing and handling your feedback.
If you don\'t want to provide any feedback, please remove this app and download the officially released version of Huawei Health instead.
By tapping AGREE, you also agree to share your data with our contracted affiliate in the Chinese mainland, who we\'ve partnered with for technical support.
